title: __len__, __getitem__, __setitem__

glossary.definition: A special built-in method in Python that has double underscores before and after its name.
Writing code in a way that is natural, concise, and follows the best practices of the Python programming language.

overview.what: In Python, using square brackets like `obj[key]` and calling `len(obj)` do not have special compiler-level behavior for custom classes. Instead, they are routed directly to specific 'dunder' (double underscore) methods on the object: `__getitem__(self, key)`, `__setitem__(self, key, value)`, and `__len__(self)`. For example, `my_list[0]` internally calls `my_list.__getitem__(0)`. Any custom class that implements these methods will seamlessly behave like a built-in list or dictionary. Analogy: Dunder methods are like standardized electrical outlets in a house. You don't need to know how the electricity is generated inside the appliance; as long as the appliance has the right plug (implements the right dunder method), you can plug it into the wall (use standard Python syntax like `len()` or `[]`).
overview.why: Implementing these methods allows custom objects to integrate natively with Python's built-in syntax. This makes your code more intuitive and 'Pythonic' by relying on standard operators instead of custom methods like `obj.get_value(key)`.

why_learning_this: Building custom container classes that behave exactly like built-in lists or dictionaries.
Understanding how data science libraries like Pandas or PyTorch allow row/column access via square brackets.
Writing intuitive, standard APIs that other Python developers already know how to use.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to raise KeyError or IndexError
common_mistakes.explanation: If `__getitem__` is asked for an invalid key or index, you must explicitly raise `KeyError` or `IndexError` instead of returning `None` or raising a generic `Exception`. Python relies on these specific errors.

common_mistakes.title: Returning the wrong type from __len__
common_mistakes.explanation: `__len__` must strictly return a non-negative integer. Returning a float, a string, or a negative number will cause Python to raise a `TypeError` or `ValueError`.

recall_questions.q: What method is called under the hood when you use square bracket syntax to read a value, like `my_obj['key']`?
recall_questions.answer: The `__getitem__` method is called with the key as its argument.

recall_questions.q: What method must a class implement for the built-in `len()` function to work on its instances?
recall_questions.answer: The class must implement the `__len__` method, which must return a non-negative integer.

recall_questions.q: Why is it generally better to implement `__getitem__` instead of creating a custom method like `get_value()`?
recall_questions.answer: It allows the custom object to integrate natively with standard Python syntax, making the code more intuitive and enabling the object to duck-type as a standard container.

practice_tasks.title: Implement a simple Dictionary Wrapper
practice_tasks.prompt: The `Config` class uses custom methods to read, set, and get the length of the data. Modify the class to use standard python syntax by replacing these methods with the appropriate `__getitem__`, `__setitem__`, and `__len__` dunder methods.

aha_moment.prediction: What happens when we access `box[10]` and `box['hello ']` even though `MagicBox` has no internal data?
aha_moment.common_guess: It raises an IndexError or KeyError because the box is empty and has no underlying list or dictionary.
aha_moment.why: `__getitem__` is simply a method call. It doesn't actually need to look up data in a dictionary or list; it receives the key inside the brackets as an argument and can return any dynamically computed value.

challenge.title: A Repeating Sequence
challenge.prompt: Create a class `Repeater` initialized with a string. Its `__len__` should return the length of the string. When accessed with `__getitem__`, it should return the character at the given index, wrapping around indefinitely if the index is larger than the string length. (Assume positive indices). For example, `Repeater('abc')[4]` should return `'b'`.

understanding_checks.question: What will the following code output?
understanding_checks.why: The square bracket syntax `box[1]` internally calls the `__getitem__` method with `1` as the `index` argument. The method accesses `self.contents[1]` which is `20`, multiplies it by `2`, and returns `40`.

understanding_checks.question: The following class is intended to allow getting its length using `len(bag)`. However, it raises a TypeError. What is wrong?
understanding_checks.answer: `__len__` must return an integer, but it is returning a string.
understanding_checks.why: Python strictly enforces that the `__len__` dunder method returns a non-negative integer. Returning a string causes a TypeError when the built-in `len()` function is called on the object.
